title: Anger At God For The Earthquake
dek: When the ground splits open and takes what you love, the fury that follows is not a failure of faith. It is the body’s most honest prayer, demanding that the divine witness the devastation.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body’s First Cry: Feeling Anger at God After the Earthquake


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.170Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake before dawn. The sky outside your window is a bruised shade of purple, the kind that stretches out quietly before the sun breaks. The air is heavy, thick with the smell of damp earth - a raw, loamy scent that seeps through cracked windowpanes - and resin from the pine trees just beyond the ridge. Your body is not yet ready for the day, but it’s already working hard. Your jaw clenches tightly, the masseter muscles on either side of your face knotted so hard they ache like old steel cables pulled taut beneath your skin. Behind your eyes, a relentless ache pulses, sharp and unforgiving, like the tremors still echoing in your bones long after the ground has settled.

In your chest, something heavy presses down, lodged deep near the heart, a jagged stone that makes your breath shallow and ragged. It is not just sorrow you carry. No, this is something fiercer, rawer: a fire licking at your diaphragm, flaring up suddenly, hot and untamed as it climbs through your ribs, scraping your throat on its way up. This anger - directed at God, at the universe, at the earth itself for betraying its promise of stability - sings a brutal, unyielding song. You cannot ignore it. Your body knows this is no moral failing. Your jaw remembers what your lips cannot say aloud.

The earth breaks open, and so do you. The tremors ripple through your spine, coiling the psoas muscles deep within your abdomen like tightly wound springs ready to snap. Your trapezius muscles rise in a defensive knot under your scalp, tightening with the weight of betrayal until it feels like a noose. This is not pathology; this is biology. The body, your flesh and fascia, tells you a story you cannot yet speak with words: rage is the first language of grief, and it demands to be heard.

## The Sacred Anger: When the Body Speaks Blasphemy

Anger aimed at God is a taboo wound, a secret many women carry deep within the silent chambers of their bodies. You were taught that fury is ugly, unladylike, a sin whispered alongside the commands to “forgive and forget.” But what if this anger is the most honest prayer you can offer? Not a plea for mercy or comfort, but a declaration of your fierce refusal to accept what has been done.

Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic psychotherapist and teacher who works intimately with trauma and the body’s wisdom, reminds us that anger is “the body’s way of mobilizing energy to set limits and protect the self.” The earthquake - the brutal rupture of the very ground beneath your feet - shatters your sense of safety, your most basic trust. Your body responds by bracing, by tightening the levator scapulae muscles that lift your shoulders toward your ears, as if shielding your heart from the weight of cosmic betrayal. This contraction, this visceral recoil, is not meant to be shamed or quieted.

In the stillness of night, when the world around you sleeps, your body carries the tremors too. Your diaphragm clenches, tightening around your lungs like a fist trying to hold back the sobs you cannot release. The tightness radiates outward, a fiery band pressed hard across your solar plexus, a visceral knot that echoes the seismic shifts beneath the earth. This is your rebellion, your body’s way of naming the nameless.

Anger at God is often called blasphemy because it breaks the silence expected of those who suffer. But this silence is a cage, and your body is the first to shatter its bars. The levator scapulae and trapezius muscles rise in stubborn resistance, the masseter clenches in defiance, and the diaphragm tightens in the belly’s core - all speaking a language older than words, older than doctrine. This is sacred anger, a fire forged in the crucible of loss, and it demands your attention.

## The Earthquake Inside: Trauma Stored in Muscle and Memory

Bessel van der Kolk, whose work on trauma transformed how we understand the body’s role in healing, teaches that “trauma is not just an event that took place long ago; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body.” The earthquake is both an external rupture and a rupture in your internal landscape. Your body holds the shock in the fascia - the dense connective tissue wrapping every muscle, bone, and organ - like a secret archive of pain.

You might notice the tightness that lingers in your psoas muscles, those deep core muscles linking your torso to your legs. The psoas, often called the “muscle of survival,” contracts whenever flight is impossible and fight seems futile. This primal muscle tenses in response to terror, and when the earth beneath you trembles, it remembers. That coil of tension is not a failure of your body but a signal, a message encoded in flesh and nerve endings saying, “Beware. Protect. Resist.”

Your masseter muscle - the jaw - clenches again and again, a clenched fist of muscle that speaks the rage you cannot yet voice. The body’s memory is stubborn; it keeps the score. The masseter, the trapezius, the levator scapulae - they hold your fury like a sacred fire burning through the night air, carrying the sharp tang of adrenaline and sweat.

The fascia itself thickens and tightens, a web of tension that resists movement, a network that remembers the shaking earth and the fear that followed. This is not something to be fixed or ignored but understood as the body’s way of holding truth. Trauma lives in the sinews and the fibers, the membranes that bind your muscles to bone and skin to soul. Your body does not lie; it carries the story in every cell.

## The Herbal Ally: Medicine for the Nervous System’s Fury

When anger surges through your body with the force of an earthquake, your nervous system becomes a furnace of heat and tension. The sympathetic nervous system - the one that prepares you to fight, freeze, or flee - is flooded with adrenaline and cortisol. Meanwhile, the parasympathetic nervous system, responsible for rest, repair, and digestion, struggles to regain control. This imbalance leaves muscles taut and breath shallow, your body perpetually primed for danger.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), the liver is the organ most intimately connected to anger. When liver qi stagnates - that is, when the smooth flow of energy is blocked - irritability, tension headaches, and a feeling of being trapped inside your own skin follow. Herbal allies that soothe the liver and calm the nervous system have been staples of healing for centuries.

One of my trusted allies in this fierce healing is Jiaogulan (Gynostemma pentaphyllum), a gentle adaptogen revered in Chinese medicine as the “herb of immortality.” Jiaogulan works on a deep level, modulating the body’s stress response without dulling your essential fire. It acts like a soft yet firm hand on the back, encouraging muscles to release and breath to deepen. Its saponins support cardiovascular health, gently balancing blood pressure and reducing inflammation that often accompanies chronic stress. When the psoas and trapezius muscles are clenched in protest, Jiaogulan’s subtle influence helps unlock those tight coils without sedation.

Another cornerstone is passionflower (Passiflora incarnata), a medicine for the nervous system’s edge. Passionflower calms the mind and eases the diaphragm’s grip, allowing the body to find a rhythm beyond the tight bands of tension. Its flavonoids gently nudge GABA neurotransmitters, which helps quiet the racing thoughts and frees the breath from its constricted cage. When your diaphragm clenches as if holding in a scream, passionflower invites it to soften, to let go.

These herbs are not sedatives designed to silence your anger or dull your body’s wisdom. Instead, they are medicines that hold you steady as you face the storm. They support the nervous system’s ability to self-regulate, to find balance amid chaos, and to embody your rage without being consumed by it. The taste of Jiaogulan is slightly sweet, earthy with a faint bitterness, a reminder that healing is never all comfort. Passionflower’s flavor is floral and delicate, a balm on the tongue like a whispered promise that the fire inside you can be tended.

I prepare these herbs as teas, steeping Jiaogulan leaves gently in hot water, letting the steam carry the scent of the forest into my kitchen. Passionflower I use as a tincture, its concentrated essence a drop or two under the tongue when the tension tightens behind my eyes or the jaw clenches like a trap. This ritual - the brewing, the tasting, the slow sipping - is itself a somatic practice, a way to ground yourself in the body’s care.

## The Wisdom of Resmaa Menakem: Racialized Trauma and the Body's Rage

Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma offers a profound lens through which to understand the body’s memory of violence and rupture. He insists that “trauma is stored in the body, not just the psyche.” While his teachings focus on social and racial trauma, the somatic truths resonate deeply with any trauma that shakes your foundation - whether personal or collective.

The earthquake is a collective trauma, a rupture threading through the bodies of communities and individuals alike. Menakem’s approach teaches us to listen with fierce attention and radical compassion to the body’s language. When you feel the tightness in your levator scapulae and trapezius muscles, when your breath catches and your heartbeat pounds in your ears, you are not alone. Your body’s rage is an invitation to ground yourself firmly in the physical.

Menakem encourages somatic practices that engage the body directly - not through words, but through movement and sensation. When words fail, the body speaks. This is how you reclaim your body from the earthquake’s aftermath: by moving through the tension stored in muscle and fascia, by feeling and releasing, by dancing with the tremors inside until they become a dance of resilience rather than terror.

His somatic exercises often involve slow, deliberate shaking or tapping of muscles to invite nervous system regulation. For example, gently shaking the hands or loosening the shoulders can signal to the brain that danger has passed. This is not a quick fix but a profound, embodied practice of safety and presence. You learn to meet your rage not as an enemy but as a messenger, a fierce protector shaped by trauma but not defined by it.

In the context of earthquake trauma, Menakem’s teachings remind us that healing cannot be rushed. The body’s memory is dense and layered, like the earth itself. It must be approached with patience, kindness, and unwavering attention.

## An Embodied Practice: Naming the Rage in Your Body

In the quiet hours before dawn, find a place where you can sit undisturbed. The air is cool, carrying the scent of damp soil and juniper needles crushed underfoot. Settle into a comfortable seat, feet flat on the floor, spine long but not rigid. Feel the weight of your body grounded through your sit bones, the solid connection to the earth beneath you.

Close your eyes and take a slow, deliberate breath in through your nose, filling the belly first, then expanding the diaphragm upward against the ribs. The breath is cool and clean, moving like a wave through your torso. As you exhale slowly through parted lips, notice where the anger lives in your body. Is it a burning heat in your solar plexus, a tightening in your masseter, a coil of resistance in your psoas? Let your attention rest on that place, soft but steady, without judgment or the urge to fix.

Begin a gentle movement: roll your shoulders slowly, letting the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles unwind with each rotation. Feel the fascia stretch and soften, the tension melting away like ice under warm sunlight. As you do this, silently name the anger: “I see you. I feel you. You are not alone.” Speak these words softly in your mind, as if to a frightened child or a wild animal cornered in the dark.

Allow your hands to rest on your belly, feeling the rise and fall of your breath. If tears come, let them fall freely. Your body knows this is not weakness but a vital part of release. When you feel ready, bring your hands to your chest, resting them over your heart. Feel the steady beating beneath your fingertips, the life pulsing beneath your skin. Breathe into the space you have created - a container for your rage, held with tenderness and respect.

This practice is not about controlling or suppressing your anger but about acknowledging and holding it, becoming an ally rather than an adversary to your own body’s wisdom. The muscles, the fascia, the breath - all become witnesses to your fury, making space for it without judgment.

## The Smell of Grief and the Taste of Fury

Anger after an earthquake carries a scent. It smells like dust and wet stone, the dry, metallic tang on the tongue that lingers after a tremor. It is the scent of crushed pine needles, the sharp bite of cold air in the early morning hours. Your body remembers these sensory details, embedding them deep in the nervous system’s memory banks.

Your own skin changes when anger lives there. There is a coppery note, a trace of adrenaline mixed with the musky heat of clenching muscles. You may notice the bitter taste of bile rising at the back of your throat, the acidic sting that accompanies rage held too long in silence or shame.

This is the body’s truth, the visceral reality many have been taught to ignore or hide. But you do not need to be ashamed. This is your earthquake inside - a trembling that demands to be witnessed, smelled, tasted, and honored.

The smell of sweat after a night of tension is different too. It is not just salty but sharp, a smell that carries the weight of fear and fury coiled in every fiber. The skin, your largest organ, remembers trauma like a map of smell and sensation, a living record of what you have endured.

When you prepare herbs like Jiaogulan and passionflower, the aromas mingle with this visceral scent. The earthy, slightly sweet fragrance of Jiaogulan tea blends with the floral notes of passionflower tincture, creating a medicine cabinet of smells that soothe the nervous system and remind the body of its capacity to heal.

## The Jaw Remembers: Compassion for Your Own Fury

Thich Nhat Hanh, the venerable Vietnamese Zen master, teaches that “to be angry is to be human.” His radical tenderness toward our most difficult emotions invites us to hold our anger with mindfulness and compassion rather than shame or fear. The jaw - the masseter muscle - remembers all the times you were told to be quiet, to swallow your fury, to smile through pain that screamed beneath the surface.

When your jaw clenches, it is the body’s way of holding your rage in place, a protective mechanism honed by years of conditioning. You can learn to soften this clenched muscle, to invite the body to release what it carries. Thich Nhat Hanh suggests a practice of “breathing in anger, breathing out peace.” With each breath, you invite your body to soften, to let go of the tight grip in the masseter and the tension in the trapezius.

This is not erasure of anger but tender acknowledgment, a way of befriending the fire inside without being burned. When you place your fingertips gently on the jaw muscles - just below the ear along the hinge of the mandible - you can feel the hardness and heat of clenched tension. Massaging softly, coaxing the muscle to relax, is itself an act of self-compassion.

When Thich Nhat Hanh talks about holding anger like a crying baby, he is speaking to the body’s need for comfort. Your rage is not a monster to be slain; it is a wounded part of you that needs to be held. The earthquake shattered your world, and your rage is a sacred response, an unspoken prayer carved in muscle, breath, and sweat.

You are not alone in this. Your body, your flesh, your fascia know the way forward - one breath, one movement, one small softening at a time.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: Is anger at God a sign of losing faith?**  
**A:** Anger at God is not a loss of faith but an expression of profound engagement with the mystery of suffering. It is an honest, embodied response to trauma and injustice. The body’s rage is a form of witness, not betrayal. This anger can be the fuel that propels you toward deeper understanding and a more authentic relationship with whatever you hold sacred. Teachers like Pema Chödrön remind us that embracing our difficult emotions, including anger, opens the door to compassion - not just for others but for ourselves.

**Q: How can I tell if my anger is healthy or destructive?**  
**A:** Healthy anger is alive in the body, felt fully, and moves through you. It leads to clear boundaries, necessary change, or protective action. Destructive anger stays stuck, causing chronic muscle tension and isolation. Notice if your masseter, psoas, and trapezius muscles remain clenched long after the triggering event has passed. Are you carrying the rage in a tight coil that never unwinds? That’s a sign of trapped anger. Paying attention to these somatic signals can help you discern whether your anger is dynamic or stagnant.

**Q: Can herbs really help me manage this kind of anger?**  
**A:** Yes. Herbs like Jiaogulan and passionflower support the nervous system’s balance and encourage muscles and fascia to relax. They do not numb or suppress your anger but provide a stable container for your body's experience. When combined with embodied practices, herbal allies help you navigate the fiery landscape of rage with more ease and clarity. As Gabor Maté notes, healing happens in the body, and plants are among the oldest teachers in this process.

**Q: What if I’m afraid to feel this anger?**  
**A:** Fear of anger is common, especially for women taught to see fury as dangerous or shameful. Start gently, with small embodied practices that honor your body’s signals. Teachers like Resmaa Menakem and Thich Nhat Hanh offer guidance on holding anger with compassion and safety. Begin by noticing where anger lives in your body without judgment. Simple movements - rolling your shoulders, softening your jaw, breathing into your belly - can create a safe container. Over time, you can build tolerance for this fierceness within you.

**Q: How do I know when the anger is “finished”?**  
**A:** Anger is not a linear process with a neat end. It ebbs and flows like the earth’s own rhythms. Healing is about learning to live with your anger in a way that feels integrated and authentic, allowing it to teach you rather than control you. As Robert Augustus Masters explains, anger is energy that must be mobilized and expressed to be transformed. When your body’s muscles begin to soften naturally, your breath deepens and slows, and you feel less reactive, you know you are moving toward integration, not erasure.

**Q: How can I support my body in releasing the trauma stored after an earthquake?**  
**A:** Engage in gentle somatic movement practices that invite your muscles and fascia to soften. This might include slow shoulder rolls, gentle neck stretches to ease the levator scapulae and trapezius, and breathing exercises that expand the diaphragm fully. Herbal allies like Jiaogulan and passionflower support this process by calming the nervous system and releasing tension. Consider working with a somatic therapist or bodyworker trained in trauma-informed care, as they can guide you through the layers of stored tension safely.

**Q: Are there other herbs or modalities that support anger held in the body?**  
**A:** Absolutely. Alongside Jiaogulan and passionflower, herbs like skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) and chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) are excellent for calming nervous system hyperarousal. In TCM, Bupleurum root (Chai Hu) is often used to move stagnant liver qi and relieve irritability. Acupuncture, cupping, and gentle massage can also help release fascia-bound tension. Remember, herbs and bodywork are not about silencing anger but helping the body find its natural rhythm and resilience.

---

The earth shakes, and so do you. Your body remembers every tremor, every fracture, every shift. Your anger is a fierce, unyielding pulse beneath the skin - a voice that demands to be heard. This is your medicine, your prayer, your rebellion. Hold it tenderly, breathe into it deeply, and let the body lead you home.

---
